steps.title: Identify the clause structure
steps.content: Left of the blank: “The book wasn't written solely by Kittredge” is an independent clause (subject + verb). Right of the blank: “other authors, such as Linda Hogan and Barry Lopez, also contributed essays” is also an independent clause (subject “other authors,” verb “contributed”). The word “however” must connect these two independent clauses and show contrast between them.

steps.title: Apply the conjunctive adverb rule
steps.content:
When a conjunctive adverb (however, therefore, moreover, thus) joins two independent clauses, the standard SAT pattern is: semicolon before the adverb, then comma after it. Choice B places the semicolon before “however” and a comma after it, creating the correct pattern: IC; however, IC. Choice A reverses this (comma before, semicolon after), which violates the rule. Choice C creates a new sentence with a period (acceptable but not the SAT's preferred format when both versions are options). Choice D uses only a comma, creating a comma splice.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
